title: Race when waiting for "not-running" when auto remove is on
body:
### Issue Description

This is a result of investigation of https://code.forgejo.org/forgejo/runner/issues/429 and https://code.forgejo.org/forgejo/runner/issues/413

Short version is - certain CI configurations run podman container with `--rm` flag set and then wait for it to exit to get exit code (Sequence of API calls is somewhat - create -> start -> attach -> wait). For some reason it depends on how long the container was running. For short runs (< 5s) it almost always succeeds, but for longer runs (>= 10s) it sometimes fails with status -1 and an empty code.

After it was bisected to PR #23646, I took some closer look at `WaitForExit` / `WaitForConditionWithInterval` functions. On successful runs `WaitForExit` returns 0, nil. While it fails, `syncContainer` (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/01feac8af51f57614033838a225f5b43a7ef178b/libpod/container_api.go#L651) returns "container has already been removed".

Since next line handles "no such container" error already, just changing the condition to `if errors.Is(err, define.ErrNoSuchCtr) || errors.Is(err, define.ErrCtrRemoved)` does the trick - `c.runtime.state.GetContainerExitCode` returns successful return code.

I didn't look deep into what causes this race, but it seems reasonable to expect that both `ErrNoSuchCtr` and `ErrCtrRemoved` are handled similarly for this use-case.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Pretty much described https://code.forgejo.org/forgejo/runner/issues/413#issuecomment-28320

However, since this is a race it may not be that easy to reproduce.

### Describe the results you received

Waiting for container which started with `--rm` returns `-1`, `nil`, even though container exited with code `0`.

### Describe the results you expected

Get actual exit code of the container.

### podman info output

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

### Additional environment details

Additional environment details

### Additional information

@Luap99

comments.body: @ytimenkov Thanks for the detailed report. Are you willing to open a PR with the fix? I agree that matching ErrCtrRemoved is the right thing there.

While preparing PR and doing more testing (basically running that workflow in a loop) I found another race in `WaitForConditionWithInterval`:
First, control falls into https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/faf8574bb47870f275cd97f5088bc5de5baa2558/libpod/container_api.go#L774 which results in sending `(-1, nil)` while at the same time, another goroutine (`WaitForExit` which I mentioned - https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/faf8574bb47870f275cd97f5088bc5de5baa2558/libpod/container_api.go#L759) sends `(0, nil)` queried from the DB.

So which one is first to send - is indeterminate (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/faf8574bb47870f275cd97f5088bc5de5baa2558/libpod/container_api.go#L829) but they lead to different outcomes. I'll try to patch it up by querying a DB (the code looks like it meant to do something in this spirit, just omitted).

Another option is to do literally nothing in this case if `waitForExit` is set. But this feels much riskier...
But `len(wantedStates)` should be 0 so we are never in that branch, unless you wait on multiple conditions. What is the actual command/API query you use to wait?

This is from my "instrumented" code:
```
ERRO[0730] WaitForConditionWithInterval(775): GetContainerExitCode: 0, state: unknown, err(2): <nil>, wantedStates: map[created:true initialized:true removing:true] 
ERRO[0730] WaitForConditionWithInterval: final result: {0 <nil>} 
@ - - [06/Mar/2025:12:02:04 +0100] "POST /v1.41/containers/16598b0740686dc7230aba6cb3b97f7b2d5f85b769aca87f7938848c97367e5f/wait?condition=not-running HTTP/1.1" 200 30 "" "Go-http-client/1.1"
ERRO[0730] WaitForExit(655): state.syncContainer - retry from db: <nil>, 0 
ERRO[0730] WaitForConditionWithInterval: c.WaitForExit got 0, <nil> 
```

(lines are ~ https://github.com/ytimenkov/podman/blob/wait-race-with-rm/libpod/container_api.go)

Ok yeah that seems to be some of the weird docker compat API mapping to our internal container states. I don't understand why it manually checks these states, the regular WaitForExit() seems more than good enough to be called for this.

What is the requirement for "not-running"? I assume it should block until the container exits, and if it is not running it return right away (unlike "next-exit"), so that matches the logic for WaitForExit()

This is a CI job executor (a reimplementation of GitHub actions) - it starts a container, executes a command in it and collects exit code to decide whether to break or continue.  Something like one-off `set -e; podman run --rm -i xxx`.

When you wrote about restarting - I realized that there is an option to reuse containers (likely a kind of optimization), but it is not enabled (it is actually forced to be disabled in the adaptation of the runner).

I don't see any obvious reason for using just "not-running". I guess it's just what first came to mind to the original author. Back when I didn't know how this part of podman works I thought that waiting should be initiated *before* starting the container to avoid race (like the one we're discussing) which now sounds like a perfect candidate for "next-exit".
Or autoremove option should be off (which has other implication, e.g. if CI job gets killed or cancelled).

To answer your "where not-running" comes from - it comes from Docker Go client API:

https://github.com/moby/moby/blob/a9c9d5bb2504f9f6d98ac8e7bce677814858b27d/api/types/container/waitcondition.go#L19

which is called from  https://github.com/nektos/act/blob/49710c85044d8049ae6e4a66d10d1fb9c5cbf56f/pkg/container/docker_run.go#L887

Yeah I know what the podman semantics are and the support values for the docker API, what I don't fully understand is the docker semantics for each state. If "not-running" really does return as soon as the container is not running then this is the perfect behavior to may to WaitForExit() without doing the other weird state checks that are quite inconsistent (as it internally just polls the state).

Regardless your fix is valid and we should merge that, I can think of the possible optimization later.
